jellyfinEnhancedRequiredMessage: The Jellyfin Enhanced plugin is required to view request/download progress.
telegramConnectError: Could not start the Telegram connection
discordConnectError: Could not start the Discord connection
channelNotConfigured: The server hasn't configured this option.
waitingConnection: Waiting for confirmation… complete the step in the other tab.
connectionTimeout: Connection not detected yet. If you completed the other tab, reopen Settings to check again.
tourStep1: Welcome to JellyNotify! Here you'll see notifications about your content requests: approvals, download progress, and when things become available.
tourStep2: Mark everything as read or clear it all with these buttons. The number on the bell tells you how many you haven't seen yet.
tourStep3: From here you choose what to be notified about, in which language, and you can connect Telegram, Discord, or WhatsApp to also get alerts outside of Jellyfin.

config.subtitle: Personal notification system for Jellyfin — Serr, Sonarr, Radarr.
config.refreshNotice: If you just installed or updated JellyNotify and don't see the notification bell, or the plugin isn't showing under Plugins, refresh the page. If a normal refresh (Ctrl+R / Cmd+R) doesn't work, try a hard refresh instead — Ctrl+Shift+R (Cmd+Shift+R on Mac), or Shift+click the browser's reload button — since your browser may have cached the page from before the plugin was installed.
config.pluginDesc: JellyNotify notifies you in real time about what's happening with your content requests: when they're created, approved or declined, how the download is going, and when it's finally available — right inside Jellyfin, and optionally on Telegram, Discord, or WhatsApp too.
config.overviewDesc: To get notified about your requests (created, approved, declined, available...), connect Seerr. To know download progress, connect Sonarr (series) and Radarr (movies). If you also want these alerts to reach you outside the server, connect a Telegram, Discord, or WhatsApp bot from the notification bell's settings panel.
config.autoLanguageDesc: Auto follows the language each Jellyfin user already has configured on the server.
config.webInjectionLabel: Enable global web injection (bell/panel in Jellyfin Web)
config.webInjectionDesc: Disable only for troubleshooting — the bell will not appear anywhere if this is off.
config.seerrWebhookUrlLabel: Webhook URL (paste this into Overseerr/Jellyseerr → Settings → Notifications → Webhook)
config.seerrWebhookNotConfiguredHint: With this on, Seerr tells JellyNotify the instant a request's status changes (approved, available, declined...). If you skip this step, JellyNotify only catches up on Seerr changes once, when Jellyfin starts — request status changes won't be noticed again until the next restart.
config.seerrWebhookPayloadHint: Only relevant if you set this up manually: you can leave Seerr's default JSON payload template as-is — just make sure it still includes the request.request_id field, which is all this plugin reads (the rest of the payload is ignored; JellyNotify always re-checks the request's current status directly with Seerr).
config.seerrWebhookWarning: Seerr needs to be able to reach this address from wherever it's running, over HTTPS with a valid certificate (a self-signed one won't work) if Seerr and Jellyfin aren't on the same local network. If your Jellyfin is only reachable on a local address like 192.168.x.x, this only works if Seerr runs on that same network too.
config.seerrNoPollingHint: Seerr notifies JellyNotify instantly via webhook — there's no polling interval to set. A single check runs once when Jellyfin starts, only to catch up on anything that changed while it was off.
config.arrPollingIntervalDesc: Only used to check download progress and catch stalled/failed downloads — Sonarr/Radarr have no webhook for that. Availability itself arrives instantly via the webhook below, regardless of this setting.
config.arrWebhookUrlLabel: Webhook URL (paste this into every Sonarr/Radarr instance you want instant delivery from, in its Settings → Connect → add a Webhook)
config.arrWebhookNotConfiguredHint: With this on, the instance tells JellyNotify the moment it grabs a release ("download started" arrives instantly, whether Sonarr/Radarr found it automatically or you searched for one by hand) and the moment it's imported ("available" arrives instantly too, with audio/subtitle languages included). If you skip this step, JellyNotify won't be told about newly available content from this instance at all — there is no fallback polling for availability, only for download progress. This same toggle and URL are shared across every Sonarr/Radarr instance — turning it on or off here does the same everywhere.
config.autoConfigureWebhookHint: Tries to configure this for you directly in Seerr using the API key above. If it can't (older version, network, permissions), nothing breaks — the Copy webhook URL button above is always there as a manual fallback.
config.arrAutoConfigureSharedHint: Tries to create this connection for you directly in this instance using its API key above, and enables the shared toggle above (affects every Sonarr/Radarr instance, same as toggling it by hand). If it can't (older version, network, permissions), nothing breaks — the Copy webhook URL button above is always there as a manual fallback.
config.webhookNeverReceived: No webhook call received yet — click Test to check.
config.notifEnabledDesc: Master switch for every notification (in-app bell and all connected bots). Turn off to pause everything without disconnecting anything.
config.dedupWindowDesc: Prevents the same notification from being sent more than once within this many minutes — it stops duplicates, it never causes repeats.
config.downloadingThresholdDesc: The mid-download "Downloading" notification is only sent once a download reaches this percentage. "Download started" still fires earlier, as soon as the transfer really begins.
config.discordWebhookHint: Created from Server Settings → Integrations → Webhooks in Discord — not the Developer Portal, which is for the next step. Example: https://discord.com/api/webhooks/123456789012345678/AbCdEfGh...
config.discordBotTokenLabel: Bot token (enables personal Discord for users)
config.discordBotTokenHint: Bot tab of the Developer Portal → Reset Token. It's a long string — copy it exactly, it's only shown once.
config.discordClientIdHint: OAuth2 tab of the Developer Portal, just a number. Example: 123456789012345678
config.discordClientSecretHint: Right next to the Client ID, same OAuth2 tab. Treat it like a password.
config.discordRedirectLabel: OAuth redirect URI (add this in Discord's OAuth2 → Redirects)
config.discordRedirectHint: Auto-filled from the address you're using right now to view this page — it does not need to be public or use HTTPS, since your own browser follows this link, Discord's servers never call it directly. If people also connect from a different address (a public domain, for example, while you're on a local IP), add that address's callback URL here too, as a separate entry in Discord's Redirects list — it must match exactly whichever one was used, or Discord will reject it.
config.discordDesc: The webhook above is a global/admin-only broadcast. The bot token + Client ID/Secret together unlock "Connect Discord" in every user's notification panel — the bot must share a server with each user it DMs (Discord's own anti-spam rule).
config.telegramTokenHint: @BotFather gives you this right after creating the bot with /newbot. Example: 123456789:AAExampleTokenXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
config.telegramUsernameHint: The username you gave the bot in BotFather, without the @ symbol. Example: MyServerBot
config.telegramChatIdHint: Only needed for the admin's own test button — each user connects their personal account from the bell without needing this. Group chat IDs start with -100.
config.telegramDetectDesc: Open a chat with your bot in Telegram, send it any message, wait a few seconds, then click Detect automatically. If nothing is found, send another message to the bot first and try again.
config.telegramDesc: Users connect from the notification panel with one click — it opens a chat with your bot, and tapping Start finishes the connection automatically (no ID to type).
config.whatsappPhoneHint: The number Meta gives you (or your production number later), in international format but without the + symbol. Example: 34612345678. The free test number itself never expires, but it can only ever message up to 5 phone numbers total, added and verified manually under Meta's API Setup → "To" — that cap doesn't go away over time, only a real verified business number removes it. Its display name also can't be changed (it will always show as a generic test number to recipients); only a verified business number can request a custom display name.
config.whatsappAccessTokenHint: Copied from WhatsApp → API Setup in Meta. The one shown there by default is temporary and expires in 24h — once it does, the bot silently stops sending anything until you replace it. For one that doesn't expire, create a System User in Meta's Business Settings and generate a permanent token from there instead.
config.whatsappPhoneNumberIdHint: This is not the phone number — it's a separate numeric ID on that same Meta page. Example: 123456789012345
config.whatsappVerifyTokenHint: You make this up yourself — it just needs to match whatever you enter in Meta's webhook config. Example: jellynotify-verify-2026
config.whatsappWebhookLabel: Webhook URL (paste this into Meta's WhatsApp → Configuration → Webhook)
config.whatsappWebhookHint: Already filled in by the plugin — nothing to type here. Save this configuration first, then copy it into Meta along with the same Verify Token above.
config.whatsappWebhookWarning: Meta needs to be able to reach this address from the internet, over HTTPS with a valid certificate (a self-signed one won't work). If your Jellyfin is only reachable on your local network (an address like 192.168.x.x or similar) or has no real certificate, this URL won't work and subscribing to the webhook will fail in Meta. You'll need to expose the server to the internet with a real domain and certificate (port forwarding, a reverse proxy, or a tunnel like Cloudflare Tunnel) before this step can be completed.
config.whatsappDesc: Without the Cloud API fields above, WhatsApp is link-only: users can message your number, but the bot can't reply automatically (no welcome message, no real notifications). Fill them in to make WhatsApp a fully working channel.
config.setupGuideSummary: How to set this up (step by step)
config.discordSetupSteps:
• Go to the Discord Developer Portal and click New Application.
  • Open the Bot tab, click Reset Token, and copy it into Bot token below.
  • Open the OAuth2 tab and copy the Client ID and Client Secret into the matching fields below.
  • Still on the OAuth2 tab, under Redirects, add the exact URL shown in OAuth redirect URI below, then save it there too.
  • Save the configuration here first, then click Invite bot to my server and add the bot to a Discord server where your Jellyfin users already are — Discord requires this before the bot can DM anyone.
  • Users can now click Connect Discord from the notification bell's settings panel.
config.telegramSetupSteps:
• Open Telegram and start a chat with @BotFather.
  • Send /newbot and follow the prompts to choose a name and a username (must end in "bot").
  • BotFather replies with a token — copy it into Bot token below.
  • Copy the bot's @username (without the @) into Bot username below.
  • Save the configuration. Users can now click Connect Telegram from the notification bell's settings panel — it opens a chat with your bot; tapping Start finishes the connection automatically.
config.whatsappSetupSteps:
• Create a free account at developers.facebook.com (Meta for Developers).
  • Click My Apps → Create App, choose the Business type, and add the WhatsApp product when prompted.
  • Open WhatsApp → API Setup. Meta gives you a free test number here — copy its Phone Number ID and the temporary Access Token into the fields below (a permanent token needs a System User, see Meta's docs; the temporary one works fine to get started but expires after 24h).
  • Also copy the test number itself (E.164, no leading +) into Admin/bot WhatsApp number below.
  • Make up any random text and put it in Verify Token below — it's just a shared password between JellyNotify and Meta, not sensitive.
  • Save the configuration here first, then go to WhatsApp → Configuration in Meta, click Edit next to Webhook, and paste in the Webhook URL shown below plus the same Verify Token you just chose.
  • Still in that Meta screen, click Manage next to "Webhook fields" and subscribe to messages.
  • On Meta's free tier you can only message up to 5 phone numbers, added manually under API Setup → "To" — add your own number there while testing. For a real server with many users, you'll need to verify your business and get a production number.
config.whatsappPhoneNumberIdPlaceholder: From Meta's WhatsApp API Setup page
config.whatsappVerifyTokenPlaceholder: Any random string — must match Meta's webhook config
